author: @GaijinPaul
text: First cousin of the capstan drive, and arguably what was once used to move optical disk drive heads, turning rotary into linear motion with amazingly low friction. Just this morning I wound a stack of figure 8's between two bits of lumber, thinking to use it as an easy and expedient 180-degree+ hinge

author: @gshingles
text:
I had a toy in the 80s that was a Magic Rings puzzle. 8 plastic squares that were held together with fishing line. You could fold them in many directions and you had to scramble it and return the picture of the rings, etc. Or as I did, just fold them repeatedly marvelling at how pieces could move relative to each other depending on which way the structure was folded. And no, I didn't break the fishing line doing this. Sadly though,  I picked it up the other day and saw a few of the lines had broken away, probably just from UV damage or something. 
Not sure if it's the same joint but it reminded me of that :)
(I just looked it up on eBay, 8 Panel 3 Ring for Rubik Master Magic Folding Puzzle Toy Ghost Hand AU)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I looked it up - what a fascinating toy!

replies.author: @gshingles
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Definitely. Well worth a look ;)

author: @OriginalMorningStar
text: Its almost 8 years to the day since I first posted that curved fold in cardstock coated with copper foil. It was a triangle, I took 3 semicircular 'bites' out of the edges so the flat triangle turned into a solid foot shape for cardware. I realised that the curves could be made fuller, and the Morning Star was born. Great to see the ripples still crossing the globe. 😅👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @EricFranklin-d2v
text: BYU fan here. If we made that hinge with a zero stretch 3mm UltraHDPE cord, and had those cords at the top and bottom, with a v groove for the round plates to remain aligned at the center, it might be very strong and keep the rolling faces from separating under force against the axis. Neat.

replies.text: very neat mate and BYU are doing some awesome work here

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: That second joint with the 2 round halves has a very nice application in robot joints. The flat flexible parts can actually be ribbon cable or flex pcb of 0,1mm material. In this way the rolling joints will never put strain on the copper conductors with the gentle controlled bending. CNC machines and 3D printers could also use them at the joints for the conductors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very good point mate - thanks for adding it

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: Ah, that is very cool! Regarding the rolling Joint idea, Kirigami (Origami + ) and robotics are so cool. I started learning about origami and folding from how NASA was packing devices and unpacking them in Satellites, but it has so many uses.  It is almost magical how it works. There are several reasons why this can be useful, but one of them is the fact that we often make our initial materials in sheets, blocks and rods, and if we can figure out how to make 3 dimensional structures using folding techniques from sheets, that saves a lot of material as opposed to milling it out etc from blocks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah absolutely mate - I am a fan of kirigami too
